A merge request is normally reviewed by both a reviewer and a maintainer in its primary category and by a maintainer in all other categories. To spread load more evenly across eligible reviewers, Danger has picked a candidate for each review slot. Feel free to [override these selections](https://about.gitlab.com/handbook/engineering/projects/#gitlab-ui) if you think someone else would be better-suited or use the [GitLab Review Workload Dashboard](https://gitlab-org.gitlab.io/gitlab-roulette/?currentProject=gitlab-ui) to find other available reviewers. To read more on how to use the reviewer roulette, please take a look at the [Engineering workflow](https://about.gitlab.com/handbook/engineering/workflow/#basics) and [code review guidelines](https://docs.gitlab.com/ee/development/code_review.html). Please consider assigning a reviewer or maintainer who is a [domain expert](https://about.gitlab.com/handbook/engineering/projects/#gitlab-ui) in the area of the merge request. Once you've decided who will review this merge request, mention them as you normally would! [Compare with previous version](/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/merge_requests/4852/diffs?diff_id=1203240463&start_sha=6e47d5dc2d9c0d1e3eb7e01c4a49c28debdacb22)

@drosse and @lvanc would you both mind having a look at this MR and letting me know what you think from a ~frontend and ~UX perspective? :pray: I've basically copied the code we already have for `panels_base.vue` and adapted it for GitLab UI.

This iteration supports:

* Title (required)
* Disclosure dropdown with customizable actions
* Slot for the visualization to render
* Loading state
* Delayed loading state
* Setting a border color
* A slot for handling errors (left vague since this is an area we want to improve the UX on)

As per https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/issues/2764#note_2236698902, it does not handle the resizing, I'll do that in a separate issue.

It is also worth noting that this iteration doesn't currently support truncating of the title, see https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/merge_requests/174846#note_2243941748 for where this currently takes place. I'd be interested to hear thoughts on how we could implement that separately. Maybe a new GitLab UI directive? :thinking:

I have created a quick integration MR if you'd like to test it locally with your GDK: https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/-/merge_requests/174846+ (I'll fill this out more once we're generally happy with the approach). Or you can use storybook in this repo.

@rob.hunt Nice work on this! Thanks for pointing out what is and isn't supported in this MR. Overall, this looks good to me. 

**question(non-blocking):** What do you think about setting a `min-width` and a `min` and `max-height` for a panel? So that we can avoid cases like this: 

![Screen_Recording_2024-12-05_at_23.05.47](/uploads/9e1791e50aebaf0dfc681d29ce800e38/Screen_Recording_2024-12-05_at_23.05.47.mov)

...and this:

![Screen_Recording_2024-12-06_at_12.01.26](/uploads/3afd3694aca8ea113964712cbba5b046/Screen_Recording_2024-12-06_at_12.01.26.mov)

> **question(non-blocking):** What do you think about setting a `min-width` and a `min` and `max-height` for a panel?

@lvanc thanks for raising this concern. I can see why we might want to apply some constraints, it's not ideal that we allow these subpar use cases. The issue we have, is that the limitations here are dependent upon the visualization and the data within it, or the grid framework, rather than the panel itself. For instance, single stat or text-based visualizations are usually going to be able to go far smaller than a line chart visualization. Or the grid framework being used might impose its own restrictions on what sizes it supports.

Therefore, the panel would need to know the restrictions of the users' visualization within it or the grid framework being used. I would argue that we would want to apply any sizing restrictions not on the panel, but on the visualization or grid framework. This could mean that we would “signal” these restrictions to the panel component, or more likely, we would set minimum sizing requirements to the visualizations and prevent visualizations from growing too small or too big.

This would need to be on a per-visualization basis so we could either:

1. Set the sizing restrictions for each visualization within GitLab UI.
    - The difficulty here is that the size of a visualization is going to be dependent upon the data too, a line chart with a large legend is going to need more space than one with two data points.
1. Add sizing restrictions to the visualization configuration within YAML configuration.
    - We would need to add a way to set restrictions within the UI of the dashboard editor to support that workflow too.
1. Leave it up to the user to identify this as a problem, since this would only affect user-defined dashboards.
    - Which means we support users tripping themselves up, even if most users would be unlikely to do that.

@rob.hunt Thanks for explaining this! 

Considering your 3 options on how to improve this experience, I think option `1.` is the way forward. "Set the sizing restrictions for each visualization within GitLab UI"

The overall goal should be to minimize the time (as much as possible) that a user is spending on resizing and rearranging panels. 

For the limitation you mention: 

> The difficulty here is that the size of a visualization is going to be dependent upon the data too, a line chart with a large legend is going to need more space than one with two data points.

In this case would we still be able to set a `min-width` for the "line chart panel type" and then let the user decide to increase the width of the panel if chart was getting clipped?

Let me know if I am not understanding this correctly.
created #3025 to continue this discussion

@lvanc I've created a [follow-up issue](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/issues/3025) for us to tackle the min/max sizing of different chart types to see if we can come up with some sane defaults :slight_smile:
@rob.hunt Amazing, thank you!
requested review from @lvanc and @drosse

**question**: Could we simplify the API here, and just render an gl-icon here and accept the icon name as a prop? If we want to keep the slot, maybe it should be named more generic to something liked `pre-title`, since nothing is stopping the consumer to render whatever component here

@drosse for our use case we need the following:

```vue
<gl-icon
  v-if="showAlertState"
  class="gl-mr-1"
  :class="alertIconClasses"
  :name="alertIcon"
  data-testid="panel-title-alert-icon"
/>
```

This means that to simplify this we would need the following props:

- `titleIconClass`
- `titleIconName`

The `data-testid` could be standardized, and we could handle the `v-if` through prop declaration.

I'm on the fence as to which approach would be better, so I'm happy to go with whatever you'd prefer.

> If we want to keep the slot, maybe it should be named more generic to something liked `pre-title`, since nothing is stopping the consumer to render whatever component here

I'm not as concerned about this. We've described the “intent” of the slot by calling it `title-icon`. It's up to us as engineers and reviewers to identify if someone is deviating from this intent, and if they are, if the reasoning is valid.
If the spec allows an icon to be prepended to the title, then I'd vote for adding the props you mentioned. If there might be other UI elements, then I'd keep a generic slot.

changed this line in [version 20 of the diff](/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/merge_requests/4852/diffs?diff_id=1207392579&start_sha=65177715430e8235638df71317a0fd4f6cf632f4#f3b9ff4a05e7aa28e846e73ae5919797c906839d_121_134)
Done! :smile:

**question**: Similarly as above, could we standardise the alert message here and only accept the alert-message as a String prop rather than a full slot? If we prefer a slot, then a generic `after-body` might be more adapt here?

@drosse I wanted to keep the “intent” here by defining the slot as `alert-message` whilst keeping how alerts are defined as pretty vague.  My reasoning for this is because we aren't [yet certain](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/issues/2764#suggested-solution) _how_ we want alerts to look within panels:

> Additionally it should enable the consumer to add alert state via props and/or slots. This is an interim solution while we work on the next panel design iteration that will have panel alerts baked in. This is needed to support the current GitLab Analytics dashboards' panel alerts.

But we aren't yet at [the point](https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-services/design.gitlab.com/-/issues/1643/) where we are defining what this alert state looks like:

> Out of scope:
> * alert states \[info, warning, danger\]
> * alert title popover & message

Additionally, we use our own `alert-popover` slot to be able to structure the alert message however we need to make the error messages more clear. For instance: https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab/blob/f884f3de2f5000b0309a2557d3adf2e21c977509/ee/app/assets/javascripts/analytics/analytics_dashboards/components/analytics_dashboard_panel.vue#L307-307. So it wouldn't be as simple, we would need _both_ props and a slot.
Fair point, let's leave it as it is then :ok_hand:

Sure, it's not a hard requirement, but one needs to work with the other, so happy to add a check for both :thumbsup:

changed this line in [version 24 of the diff](/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/merge_requests/4852/diffs?diff_id=1211158965&start_sha=47ddeb3294e1da841afa9d8a9102e84dcee78207#f3b9ff4a05e7aa28e846e73ae5919797c906839d_12_0)
:shrug: happy with either, changed :thumbsup:

**question:** Since we already check `tooltip?.description` and `tooltip?.descriptionLink` in the computeds, do we really care if `tooltip` has some extra keys? Maybe we can get rid of this validator?

Maybe this should also be named `popover` since it's not really a tooltip :sweat_smile:

changed this line in [version 24 of the diff](/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/merge_requests/4852/diffs?diff_id=1211158965&start_sha=47ddeb3294e1da841afa9d8a9102e84dcee78207#f3b9ff4a05e7aa28e846e73ae5919797c906839d_52_0)
Good point. I've renamed this to `titlePopover` and removed the validator :thumbsup:

**question:** Do we really need `editing` prop? It's only used in `shouldShowActions` and maybe we should just use `actions` and require that the client handle the right business logic of whether or not these should be present.

I can foresee a situation where I'm not editing but still want some actions to show up. WDYT?

changed this line in [version 24 of the diff](/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/merge_requests/4852/diffs?diff_id=1211158965&start_sha=47ddeb3294e1da841afa9d8a9102e84dcee78207#f3b9ff4a05e7aa28e846e73ae5919797c906839d_80_0)

That's an excellent point! Now I think about it, I agree :100:. We should leave the `actions` logic up to the consumer of the component, and leave this component unbiased. I can also see being able to “clone” a panel on a dashboard outside the editing process.

**question:** I'm noticing a scrollbar show up on this story when running storybook locally:

@rob.hunt Should we include this case in https://gitlab.com/gitlab-org/gitlab-ui/-/issues/3025+?

:see_no_evil: oops. I've updated this to have a minimum height to prevent the scrolling. Turns out it's also kind of dependant upon your screen density/resolution too, as I found that on my Mac's retina I didn't have a scrollbar, but on my ultrawide I do :thinking:. Hoping the min height fixes :all-the-things:
This looks to be fixed on my end. Thanks @rob.hunt!

Sorry for the delay here @rob.hunt! I was OOO for a bit. Looking at this now! :eyes:

**question (minor, non-blocking):** I wonder why we went with `return this.title?.length > 0` instead of simply `return Boolean(this.title)`? There's some subtle weirdness with checking the length this way:

1. If `title` is `null`ish, it ends up creating a `null > 0` which forces a type coercion situation.
2. (incredibly nitpick-y) If `title` is truthy, it's possible that `.length` isn't actually stored next to the data and so it has to calculate the whole `.length`.

@drosse would you be up for putting together a quick follow-up to polish this up? I'm also happy to :smile:
Good catch! Let me clean this up :broom:
